description:
You are a capacity-planner who has just been handed several hourly resource-usage CSV files that live under
 /home/user/capacity/raw/.
 Your job is to transform those raw measurements into a compact, machine-readable summary that another
 reporting system will later ingest.

 1. Source data that already exists
    • /home/user/capacity/raw/serverA.csv  
    • /home/user/capacity/raw/serverB.csv  

    Every file in /home/user/capacity/raw/ is guaranteed to have exactly the same comma-separated format:

        timestamp,cpu_pct,mem_mb
        2023-07-01T00:00:00,55.5,2048
        …

    (header in the first line, then ISO-8601 timestamp, floating-point CPU %, integer RAM in MiB).

 2. What you must produce
    Create a new directory  /home/user/capacity/analysis/   (if it does not yet exist) and write all summary
    artefacts there.

    a) /home/user/capacity/analysis/summary.csv  
       Required format (the automated test will match this **exact** header and field order):

           server,average_cpu_pct,max_cpu_pct,average_mem_mb,max_mem_mb,sample_count
           <server-name>,<avg-cpu-2dp>,<max-cpu>,<avg-mem-2dp>,<max-mem>,<count>
           …

       • average_cpu_pct  and  average_mem_mb  must always be printed with exactly two digits after the
         decimal point (e.g. 62.70).  
       • max fields may be left un-padded (e.g. 70.2 or 1150).  
       • sample_count is an integer equal to the number of data rows found for that server.

    b) Per-server JSON files  
       For every raw *.csv file, create one JSON file with the same stem name inside
       /home/user/capacity/analysis/.  
       For example:  /home/user/capacity/analysis/serverA.json  

       Required JSON structure (whitespace is ignored by the test, field order is irrelevant):

       {
         "server":          "<server-name>",
         "average_cpu_pct": <float>,      // same value as in summary.csv
         "max_cpu_pct":     <float>,
         "average_mem_mb":  <float>,
         "max_mem_mb":      <integer>,
         "sample_count":    <integer>
       }

    c) A process log  
       Path: /home/user/capacity/logs/process.log (make the directory if missing).  
       Each log line must consist of an ISO-8601 timestamp, a single space, then the message text.
       For example:

           2023-09-14T12:34:56Z Processing started for serverA with 4 samples

       The log must contain, at minimum, the following five messages in this exact textual form
       (timestamps, of course, will vary):
           • “Processing started for serverA with 4 samples”
           • “Processing completed for serverA”
           • “Processing started for serverB with 4 samples”
           • “Processing completed for serverB”
           • “Summary files generated”

 3. Functional requirements
    • Derive every metric exclusively from the data present in /home/user/capacity/raw/*.csv; do not hard-code any numbers.
    • You may use any standard Linux command-line utilities or scripting languages available in a minimal
      Debian/Ubuntu environment (e.g. awk, sed, grep, python3, perl, jq, parallel, xargs, etc.).
      Feel free to run transformations for multiple files in parallel if you like; the check only cares
      about the final artefacts.
    • All newly created files and directories must be owned by the current unprivileged user and be world-readable.

 When you are done, there must therefore be exactly three new files in /home/user/capacity/analysis/
 (summary.csv, serverA.json, serverB.json) plus the log file in /home/user/capacity/logs/.

 The automated tests will:
    1. Confirm that /home/user/capacity/analysis/summary.csv exists and its contents match the format above.
    2. Parse each JSON file and compare every value with those in summary.csv.
    3. Verify that process.log contains the five required messages (string match, timestamp ignored).

 Do not display the raw CSV contents in your final answer; the grading harness already knows them.
 Demonstrate all intermediate shell output normally—this is a real terminal session.
truth:
<!-- Pre-existing raw data -->
 Directory /home/user/capacity/raw/ exists with two files:

 1) /home/user/capacity/raw/serverA.csv
    timestamp,cpu_pct,mem_mb
    2023-07-01T00:00:00,55.5,2048
    2023-07-01T01:00:00,65.0,2100
    2023-07-01T02:00:00,70.2,2200
    2023-07-01T03:00:00,60.1,2000

 2) /home/user/capacity/raw/serverB.csv
    timestamp,cpu_pct,mem_mb
    2023-07-01T00:00:00,35.2,1024
    2023-07-01T01:00:00,45.5,1100
    2023-07-01T02:00:00,50.0,1150
    2023-07-01T03:00:00,40.3,1080

 Expected outputs the checker will look for:

 A) /home/user/capacity/analysis/summary.csv
    server,average_cpu_pct,max_cpu_pct,average_mem_mb,max_mem_mb,sample_count
    serverA,62.70,70.2,2087.00,2200,4
    serverB,42.75,50.0,1088.50,1150,4

 B) /home/user/capacity/analysis/serverA.json
    {
      "server": "serverA",
      "average_cpu_pct": 62.70,
      "max_cpu_pct": 70.2,
      "average_mem_mb": 2087.00,
      "max_mem_mb": 2200,
      "sample_count": 4
    }

 C) /home/user/capacity/analysis/serverB.json
    {
      "server": "serverB",
      "average_cpu_pct": 42.75,
      "max_cpu_pct": 50.0,
      "average_mem_mb": 1088.50,
      "max_mem_mb": 1150,
      "sample_count": 4
    }

 D) /home/user/capacity/logs/process.log must include **all** of the following substrings
    (timestamps may differ, messages must match exactly):
       Processing started for serverA with 4 samples
       Processing completed for serverA
       Processing started for serverB with 4 samples
       Processing completed for serverB
       Summary files generated
